skill.name: operating-k8s-local
skill.description: Guides local Kubernetes development with Minikube, kubectl, local image workflows, service access, manifests, and debugging commands.
skill.summary: Run and debug local Kubernetes clusters with Minikube and kubectl.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns, but they are Markdown examples for Minikube and kubectl workflows rather than hidden executable code. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. The skill still needs a medium-risk warning because several examples can mutate local shell state, Kubernetes resources, and /etc/hosts.

medium_findings.title: Local and cluster-changing command examples
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are true positives as operational examples. They are legitimate for a local Kubernetes skill, but commands such as minikube start, docker build, kubectl apply, kubectl edit, and kubectl exec can change local state or cluster resources if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown code blocks and clearly match local Kubernetes operations. They are not hidden execution, but they can mutate developer and cluster state.

medium_findings.title: Privileged hosts file modification
medium_findings.description: The sudo finding is a true positive because the example appends a Minikube host entry to /etc/hosts. This is a common local-development step, but it uses elevated privileges and can persist an incorrect host mapping.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly pipes output into sudo tee -a /etc/hosts. The intent is legitimate local networking, but the privileged filesystem change is real.

low_findings.title: Static high-risk keyword matches are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance hits are false positives. The cited lines are frontmatter text, kubectl status examples, resource metrics examples, or validation messages, not cryptographic code or reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain documentation examples and string checks only. No hashing, encryption, password cracking, or reconnaissance implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Internal service URL example is not data exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL finding is a false positive. The URL targets an in-cluster sample service for network debugging and does not send secrets or contact an external domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is http://my-service:80 inside a kubectl exec debugging example. No external endpoint or sensitive payload is shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell eval with command substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends eval with minikube docker-env output. This is standard Minikube usage, but users should only run it when they trust the local minikube binary and understand it changes Docker environment variables.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly present in shell examples. It is legitimate for Minikube but still executes generated shell content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive shell into Kubernetes pods
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes kubectl exec examples that open shells or run network tools inside pods. This is expected debugging guidance, but it can expose container environments if used against sensitive workloads.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and operationally meaningful. The risk depends on the target cluster, so it is moderate rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Local Kubernetes setup can be slow when commands, image handling, and service access are scattered across references. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused Minikube and kubectl playbook for development workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides Minikube startup, profile, addon, tunnel, and service access commands.
Shows kubectl context, inspection, logging, editing, scaling, rollout, and exec workflows.
Documents local Docker image loading through Minikube Docker environment commands.
Includes Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, and Ingress manifest examples.
Gives quick checks for pod startup, crash logs, network issues, and resource usage.
limitations: Targets local Minikube clusters, not managed cloud Kubernetes production environments.
Does not automate command execution; users must review and run commands themselves.
Uses generic placeholder names that must be adapted to each application.
Includes privileged local networking guidance that should be reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap a local cluster
use_cases.description: Start Minikube, enable common addons, and prepare service access for a development application.

use_cases.title: Debug local Kubernetes workloads
use_cases.description: Inspect pods, review events, follow logs, open pod shells, and check resource usage.

use_cases.title: Prepare teaching examples
use_cases.description: Use simple manifests and commands to explain core Kubernetes objects in a local lab.

prompt_templates.title: Start Minikube
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the operating-k8s-local skill to give me the basic Minikube commands for starting a local cluster and checking status.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a local image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the operating-k8s-local skill to outline how I can build a Docker image into Minikube and deploy it with kubectl.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a failing pod
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the operating-k8s-local skill to help me investigate a pod that is not starting in my local Minikube cluster.

prompt_templates.title: Design a local workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the operating-k8s-local skill to create a local Kubernetes development workflow with addons, image builds, manifests, ingress, and debugging checks.

output_examples.input: I need to run my API locally on Kubernetes.
output: A short Minikube startup sequence with resource settings and addons.
Guidance for building the API image into Minikube.
A deployment and service plan using local image pull behavior.
A troubleshooting checklist for pod description, recent events, previous logs, and resource usage.
Suggested kubectl commands to identify crash causes.
Notes about checking readiness and liveness probes.

output_examples.input: I want to access my local app through ingress.
output: Steps to enable the ingress addon and define a host rule.
A reminder to review any hosts file changes before using elevated privileges.
Service access alternatives such as NodePort, tunnel, or port forwarding.

best_practices: Review every command before running it, especially commands that change contexts, manifests, or local host settings.
Use local-only image tags and image pull settings to avoid accidental registry pulls.
Verify the active kubectl context before applying, editing, scaling, or deleting resources.
anti_patterns: Do not run the examples against production clusters without adapting them and checking the active context.
Do not copy placeholder secrets or passwords into real workloads.
Do not append hosts file entries with sudo unless the hostname and Minikube IP are correct.

faq.question: Does this skill run Kubernetes commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance and examples for the user or agent to review.

faq.question: Is it intended for production clusters?
faq.answer: No. The content is focused on local Minikube development and testing.

faq.question: Which tools does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Minikube, kubectl, Docker image builds for local use, and basic Kubernetes manifests.

faq.question: Can it help with networking issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes service access, port forwarding, ingress, DNS checks, and simple in-cluster HTTP checks.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: Some examples change cluster resources or local system files, so users should review them before execution.

faq.question: Does it include cloud Kubernetes deployment steps?
faq.answer: No. It references local Minikube workflows and does not cover managed cloud cluster deployment.
